1: Daniel 4 is unique in the OT: it is cast as a first-person royal letter (igeret) from Nebuchadnezzar to his entire empire, announcing his own humiliation and restoration. No Babylonian parallel to such a document has been identified. A text known as the "Prayer of Nabonidus" (4Q242, found at Qumran) describes a similar illness suffered by a Babylonian king; some scholars see it as a historically dislocated tradition related to this account.
2: "Signs and wonders" (atayya umofeta'ya) — the vocabulary of the exodus transplanted to a Babylonian context. The theological claim is that the God of Israel's sovereignty extends into Babylonian experience, reversing the implied theology of conquest (that Babylon's gods defeated Israel's God, Daniel 1:2).
3: "His kingdom is an everlasting kingdom; his dominion endures from generation to generation" — the doxology here (repeated and expanded in Daniel 4:34-35) forms the literary frame of the chapter. The same language is cited or echoed in NT eschatology (Revelation 11:15) and later Jewish liturgy (Adon Olam).
4: "At rest... and thriving in my palace" — Nebuchadnezzar describes the pinnacle of success immediately before catastrophe. The pattern — prosperity followed by disaster — is a wisdom-literature topos (cf. Job 1; Ps 73). The Aramaic rachan ("flourishing, leafy") uses tree imagery that will dominate the chapter.
5: The dream-report structure parallels Daniel 2: disturbing dream, court wise men unable to interpret, Daniel brought in to succeed. The repetition reinforces the pattern that only divine revelation can access the meaning of history, not professional divination however sophisticated.
6: The summoning of all the court interpreters — magicians, astrologers, Chaldeans, soothsayers — follows the established Daniel pattern (Daniel 2, 4, 5). The reader anticipates their failure. The repetition is pedagogical: it establishes a reliable pattern before applying it to Belshazzar in Daniel 5.
7: "They could not give the interpretation" — the wise men's failure is the narrative gap that Daniel fills. The theology: true understanding of history belongs to the God who acts in it, not to human divinatory systems however refined.
8: Daniel arrives last (le'acharon). His Babylonian name Belteshazzar is glossed as "after the name of my god" — likely Bel (Marduk), chief Babylonian deity. Nebuchadnezzar acknowledges "the spirit of the holy gods" (ruach elahin qadishin) in Daniel — using his own theological framework rather than Israel's.
9: "No mystery is too difficult for you" (kol-raz la anesh lak) — raz (mystery, secret) is a Persian loanword occurring 9 times in Daniel 2 and 4. It denotes not merely a puzzle but a revealed divine secret inaccessible to human investigation. The word recurs in the Dead Sea Scrolls as a key term for divine revelation.
10: The cosmic tree vision draws on the widespread ANE image of the axis mundi, the world-tree connecting earth and heaven. Similar imagery appears in Ezek 31 (the cedar of Lebanon representing Egypt) and Ezek 17 (the eagle and the vine). In each case, the cosmic tree symbolizes imperial arrogance that is eventually cut down.
11: "Its top reached to heaven" (rageh leshemaya) — the same idiom used of the Tower of Babel (Genesis 11:4, "a tower with its top in the heavens"). The echo is deliberate: Babylonian imperial ambition repeatedly overreaches into the divine sphere and is repeatedly brought low.
12: The tree provides food, shelter, and beauty for all creation. This is the image of an ideal king as provider and protector; Nebuchadnezzar has been this for his empire. The critique is not that he is a bad king but that he has forgotten the source of his greatness.
13: "A watcher, a holy one" ('ir w'qadish) — the term 'ir ("watcher") is unique in the OT to this chapter (Daniel 4:13,17,23). It appears frequently in Second Temple apocalyptic literature, especially 1 Enoch, where the Watchers are angelic beings (some fallen). Here the watcher is holy and acts as a divine agent of judgment. The term was evidently familiar to the original audience.
14: The watcher's decree uses the language of siegecraft and military destruction: cut down, trim, strip, scatter. The command is passive (kiruhu, "cut it down"), implying divine agency through unspecified means — the empire falls, but no specific human adversary is named as God's instrument at this point.
15: "But leave the stump of its roots" (ikkar shoreshoi) — the preserved stump indicates reformative rather than final judgment. Iron and bronze binding is obscure; some read it as a symbol of constrained reason during madness, others as a physical image for the enduring dynastic claim that will be restored after the judgment period.
16: "Let his mind be changed from a human mind and let the mind of an animal be given to him, and let seven seasons pass over him." The phrase is clinically precise: lebbeh min-enasha yeshteneh (human heart/mind replaced by an animal's). Modern psychiatry labels the described condition boanthropy — the delusion of being a bovine animal. Documented cases exist from Antiquity to the present. "Seven seasons" ('iddan) most likely means seven years.
17: "This sentence is decreed by the watchers and commanded by the holy ones, so that the living may know that the Most High rules the kingdom of men and gives it to whom he will." The explicit theological purpose of the judgment is stated: the exercise of divine sovereignty over human empires. This is the thematic thesis of the entire book of Daniel.
18: Nebuchadnezzar's appeal to Daniel is a concession of his own court's inadequacy. "Because the spirit of the holy gods is in you" — despite the pagan framing, the king recognizes a category of access to divine knowledge that his own diviners lack. The repeated emphasis on Daniel's superiority to the court wise men is the book's apologetic for divine revelation over human wisdom systems.
19: Daniel's dismay (tiwhah Daniel ki sha'ah chada, "Daniel was appalled for a time") reflects genuine concern for the king. This detail humanizes Daniel and resists a reading of the chapter as simple nationalist celebration of Babylonian downfall. Daniel wishes the dream applied to Nebuchadnezzar's enemies, not to the king himself — a compassion that complicates the text's politics.
20: The interpretation begins: "The tree you saw... it is you, O king." The direct move from symbol to referent is characteristic of Daniel's interpretive style (cf. Daniel 2:36-45). Ezek 31 used the same cosmic-tree imagery for Pharaoh; the prophetic convention was established before Daniel appropriates it.
21: Daniel rehearses the tree's positive attributes before announcing judgment — acknowledging what has been good before pronouncing what must change. This mirrors prophetic oracle structure: the indictment is more powerful for being preceded by acknowledgment of genuine greatness.
22: "It is you, O king, who have grown and become strong... your dominion extends to the ends of the earth." The description is historically grounded: Nebuchadnezzar (reigned 605-562 BCE) built the Neo-Babylonian empire to its peak, including the Ishtar Gate, the Hanging Gardens, and extensive canal systems. His building inscriptions repeatedly claim that Marduk gave him power to build Babylon. The sin is not the greatness itself but the refusal to acknowledge its source.
23: The watcher-decree is repeated (from Daniel 4:13-15) — the repetition confirms the matter is fixed. In Genesis 41:32 Joseph explicitly explains that Pharaoh's double dream means "the thing is fixed by God, and God will shortly bring it about." Daniel's repetition of the watcher's command functions similarly as rhetorical confirmation that the judgment will not be reversed.
24: "It is the decree of the Most High that has come upon my lord the king." Daniel uses the respectful address "my lord the king" while delivering a judgment oracle. The combination of prophetic boldness and royal courtesy is Daniel's consistent manner throughout the book, distinguishing him from confrontational prophets like Elijah.
25: The specific content: seven years of madness, eating grass, drenched with dew. The historical basis is unconfirmed in Babylonian records, though the Nabonidus Chronicle describes Nabonidus's extended absence in Arabia at Teima — some scholars propose a partial historical connection. The purpose explicitly stated: "until you know that the Most High rules the kingdom of men."
26: "Your kingdom will be restored to you as soon as you acknowledge that Heaven rules" — the Aramaic shemayah shalit ("Heaven rules") uses Heaven as a circumlocution for God. This is one of the earliest clear attestations of this theological convention, which becomes standard in Second Temple Judaism and produces Matthew's "kingdom of heaven" as distinct from the other Synoptics' "kingdom of God."
27: Daniel's counsel — repent, show mercy to the oppressed — is an OT appeal to repentance as a means of averting decreed judgment. The conditional "it may be that there will be a prolonging" is prophetically honest: the decree is made, but God is responsive to repentance. The sins named (unrighteousness, oppression) are social justice violations — the same sins denounced by Amos and Isaiah alongside idolatry.
28: "All this came upon King Nebuchadnezzar" — the fulfillment narrated in two Aramaic words. Brevity is emphasis: despite the warning, despite the grace period, the decree executes. The narrative offers no indication of any attempt at repentance between Daniel 4:27 and Daniel 4:28.
29: Twelve months between the warning and the judgment. The specific duration suggests a grace period — divine patience before execution. This is consistent with the OT pattern of warning before judgment (Amos 4:6-12; Jeremiah 25:3-11) and with the NT's characterization of divine forbearance (Romans 2:4; 2 Peter 3:9).
30: "Is this not great Babylon, which I myself have built as a royal residence by my mighty power and for the glory of my majesty?" Nebuchadnezzar's building inscriptions from Babylon read almost identically: "I, Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon... the noble prince, who is dear to the god Marduk, I built..." The pride is not literary invention; it is historically characteristic of ANE royal ideology. The sin is attributing to oneself what belongs to the One who gave the power.
31: "While the word was still in the king's mouth, a voice fell from heaven" — the immediacy of divine judgment is absolute. No gap between pride and consequence. The "voice from heaven" (qal min shemayah) becomes bath qol ("daughter of a voice") in later rabbinic Hebrew — a technical term for divine communication after the closure of prophecy.
32: The decree is repeated a third time, adding the purpose clause: "until you know that the Most High rules the kingdom of men." The judgment is explicitly restorative and revelatory, not merely punitive. This is the pattern of OT exile theology: judgment is designed to produce knowledge of God (Ezekiel 36:23; Isaiah 45:3).
33: "That same hour the word was fulfilled against Nebuchadnezzar." The madness is described with clinical detail: eating grass like an ox, body wet with dew, hair grown like eagles' feathers, nails like birds' claws — suggesting outdoor, homeless conditions lasting years. Babylon's bureaucratic machinery apparently continued without him; cuneiform records from this period show administrative continuity that did not depend on continuous royal presence.
34: "At the end of the appointed time, I lifted my eyes toward heaven, and my understanding returned to me." The restoration is linked to a single posture: lifted eyes — an act of acknowledgment and prayer. The first-person voice returns (absent since Daniel 4:27) as sanity returns. The structural parallel between lifting eyes and restored sanity is deliberate theology: true perception of reality begins with orientation toward God.
35: "No one can stay his hand or say to him, 'What are you doing?'" — language that resonates with Job 9:12 and Isaiah 29:16, and resurfaces in Romans 9:20. Nebuchadnezzar has arrived at a theology of absolute divine sovereignty that OT Israel struggled to maintain consistently. The dramatic irony is that a pagan king now articulates what Israel's own prophets had been proclaiming for centuries.
36: The restoration narrative is compressed: reason returned, honor restored, counselors sought him out, kingdom re-established, greatness added. The speed of restoration without resistance implies either literary convention or genuine political loyalty. The administration's ability to restore a king after a prolonged absence of uncertain nature is remarkable and narratively unexplained.
37: "Now I, Nebuchadnezzar, praise and exalt and glorify the King of heaven, for all his works are true and his ways are just; and those who walk in pride he is able to humble." The closing doxology is the theological thesis of the chapter and, arguably, of the book's first half. Whether this represents genuine conversion or a literary vehicle for theological statement remains debated; the text presents it as genuine and gives it the last word.
